planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in late winter to early spring; in zone 8–9 plant December–February for best root establishment before summer heat. Climax is an early-season rabbiteye that often ripens before Premier, making it the first choice for the earliest harvest in zone 8–9. Plant with Premier, Brightwell, or Tifblue for cross-pollination.

directions: Plant in acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained, sandy or loamy soil amended with pine bark or peat in full sun; Climax is one of the original rabbiteye blueberry cultivars released by the USDA and University of Georgia, selected for very early ripening — typically the first rabbiteye to color in a planting, beginning to ripen in late May in zone 8–9. The medium-sized, firm, light blue berries have good flavor but a somewhat thicker skin than later-season varieties; this firmness improves shipping quality. Climax's moderate chilling requirement (350–400 hours below 45°F) ensures reliable bloom in zone 8–9 without risk of late-season freeze damage. The upright, vigorous shrubs are productive and long-lived. Mulch heavily with pine bark, maintain acid fertilization with ammonium sulfate, and keep soil consistently moist through the production season.
sources: University of Georgia Extension — Rabbiteye Blueberry Cultivars
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Blueberry Cultivar Selection
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Rabbiteye Blueberry Breeding

planting.note: Plant in late winter to early spring; in zone 8–9 plant December–February. Tifblue is a mid-to-late season variety that requires cross-pollination with an early-season variety (Premier, Climax) for maximum fruit set. Its larger chilling requirement (500–600 hours) compared to other rabbiteyes makes it slightly less suited to zone 9a's mild winters than Premier or Brightwell.

directions: Plant in acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained soil amended with pine bark in full sun; Tifblue was the rabbiteye blueberry standard for decades — a large, vigorous, upright shrub with good berries and excellent adaptability. Released by the USDA-ARS Tifton, Georgia station, it produces medium to large, light blue, firm, mild-flavored berries in late June to July, extending the harvest season after early and mid-season varieties have finished. The berries have excellent shelf life due to their firm texture. However, Tifblue's higher chilling requirement (500–600 hours) makes it a less reliable choice in the warmer parts of zone 8–9 where chilling hours may not accumulate sufficiently in mild winters. Best suited to zone 6–8 where more consistent chilling ensures reliable bloom and full fruit set.
sources: University of Georgia Extension — Rabbiteye Blueberry History and Cultivars
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Blueberry Cultivar Evaluation
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Vaccinium virgatum Breeding

planting.note: Plant in late winter to early spring; in zone 8–9 plant December–February for best establishment. Alapaha is an early-season rabbiteye that ripens slightly ahead of mid-season varieties, extending the harvest window at the beginning of the season. Plant with Brightwell, Premier, or Climax for cross-pollination.

directions: Plant in acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), well-drained sandy or loamy soil with pine bark amendment in full sun; Alapaha is a University of Georgia-released rabbiteye blueberry selected for exceptional berry quality — the fruit is large, light blue, and has a dessert quality with a sweet-tart balance and crisp texture that earned it high consumer preference ratings in taste trials. Released in 2001, Alapaha is among the newer rabbiteye cultivars with improved fruit quality over the older varieties developed in the mid-20th century. It ripens early in the rabbiteye season (late May to mid-June in zone 8–9), overlapping with Premier and producing well with 300–400 chilling hours. The moderately vigorous, upright-to-spreading shrubs are productive and show reasonable resistance to late-season water stress.
sources: University of Georgia Extension — Alapaha Rabbiteye Blueberry Release
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Rabbiteye Blueberry Varieties
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Vaccinium Cultivar Development

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring; Chinese bayberry prefers slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained soil and tolerates partial shade. In zone 7b–9 the tree is root-hardy and fully perennial; the top may suffer cold damage in severe zone 7 winters. Females require a male pollinator tree nearby. Trees fruit beginning at 5–7 years from transplant; produce large crops in June–July.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Chinese bayberry (yang mei, yumberry) is one of China's most celebrated fruits — a small, deep red to purplish, lychee-like berry on a large, shade-tolerant evergreen tree that is almost unknown in American gardens despite growing well in zone 7–9. The fruits, borne in June–July, are intensely flavored with a sweet-tart taste similar to a cross between pomegranate and raspberry, with a slightly grainy texture around a central pit. Chinese bayberry is rich in anthocyanins and vitamin C. The tree is dioecious — female trees bear fruit when pollinated by a male; most nurseries sell sexed trees. It grows slowly to 25–35 feet but can be kept smaller with pruning. The dark, glossy, evergreen foliage is attractive year-round.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension — Chinese Bayberry for California
USDA National Agroforestry Center — Morella rubra

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in fall or early spring before new growth starts; river birch transplants best when young. In zone 8–9 plant October–February for establishment before summer heat. Dura-Heat is notably more heat and drought tolerant than standard river birch and shows better heat resistance than Heritage birch in the hottest parts of the Gulf South.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in moist, slightly acidic, well-drained to periodically flooded soil; Dura-Heat is a heat-tolerant selection of native river birch (Betula nigra) selected at the University of Alabama for superior performance in the hot, humid summers of zone 7–9. The exfoliating, creamy-to-salmon bark that peels in papery sheets is the tree's primary ornamental feature, providing year-round interest — the effect is most dramatic on multi-trunk specimens. Unlike most birches, which require cool summers, Dura-Heat thrives in zone 9's climate and tolerates temporary flooding, making it valuable along streams, ponds, and in rain gardens. Fall color is a modest yellow. The tree is relatively fast-growing (2–3 feet per year when young) and provides dappled shade suitable for understory plantings.
sources: University of Alabama Horticulture — Dura-Heat Birch Introduction
North Carolina State Extension — Betula nigra Cultivars
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Birch Trees for Florida
